revision.comment: Bot: mark 2 deleted QID(s) as DELETED_QID in ill templates [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/24155476638|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Bungo-kuni-fudoki]][[ja:豊後国風土記]][[zh:豐後國風土記]]
'''Bungo no Kuni Fudoki''' (豊後国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Bungo Province|en|Bungo Province|qid=Q864148|lt=Bungo Province|1=Bungo Province}} (approximately modern-day {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|qid=Q133924|lt=Ōita Prefecture|1=Ōita Prefecture}}) compiled during the early {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}. It is one of the five extant Fudoki. == Overview == The exact compilation date of the ''Bungo no Kuni Fudoki'' is unknown, but the inclusion of articles closely aligned with events described in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Chronicles of Japan|1=Nihon Shoki}} (''Nihon Shoki'') suggests that it was compiled between 720 and approximately 740. Recent studies indicate that, given the similarities in form to the {{ill|Izumo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, completed in {{ill|Tenpyō 5|ja|天平5年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō 5|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135551113|1=733}} (733), ''Bungo no Kuni Fudoki'' was likely compiled around the same time.<ref>{{PDFlink|[http://ir.lib.hiroshima-u.ac.jp/metadb/up/ZZT00001/OitakenChihoshi_155_1.pdf ''A Preliminary Study of the Bungo Fudoki'']|qq=}} by Motonichi Nishibeppu, ''Oita Prefecture Local History,'' No. 155, pp. 1–20.</ref> Although the identity of the compiler is unknown, it is believed that the {{ill|Dazaifu|en|Dazaifu|qid=Q1179277|lt=Dazaifu|1=Dazaifu}} government was deeply involved. One theory posits that {{ill|Fujiwara no Umakai|en|Fujiwara no Umakai|qid=Q1370638|lt=Fujiwara no Umakai|1=Fujiwara no Umakai}}, who assumed office as the ''Saikaidō'' military governor (''Sesshō-shi'') in 732, supervised the rapid compilation of Fudoki across Kyushu, completing it in just ten months. ''Bungo no Kuni Fudoki'' is one of the few Fudoki to retain a semblance of its original format. While its existence, along with the ''Izumo-no-Kuni Fudoki,'' has been known since the early modern period, the surviving manuscripts lack many sections. The extant versions are largely considered abridged copies, containing the least material among the five extant Fudoki. Some scholars argue that it is a later fabrication, citing the brevity of the text and alignment with ''Nihon Shoki.'' Over 100 manuscript copies of ''Bungo no Kuni Fudoki'' have been identified. The original source is believed to be a manuscript preserved by the {{ill|Reizei family|en|Reizei family|qid=Q3065463|lt=Reizei family|1=Reizei family}} and confirmed in 1980. This manuscript includes a colophon dating to {{ill|Einin 5|ja|永仁5年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Einin 5|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135551114|1=1297}} (1297). The text gained wider recognition through the woodblock edition by {{ill|Arakida Hisao|ja|荒木田久老|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakida Hisao|1=Arakida Hisaoyu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q6535042}} in {{ill|Kansei 12|ja|寛政12年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 12|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135551115}} (1800) and the annotated edition ''Senchaku Bungo Fudoki'' by {{ill|Karahashi Kizan|ja|唐橋君山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karahashi Kizan|1=Karahashi Kunzan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q30935478}} (known for compiling the ''Bungo Kuni Shi'') in {{ill|Bunka 1|ja|文化元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunka 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135551117}} (1804). == Structure and Content == The text opens with an account of the origin of the province's name, followed by explanations of the names and traditions of each district: Hita, Kusu, Naoiri, Ōno, Ama, Ōita, Hayami, and Kunisaki. Many of the place names are attributed to the Kyushu imperial tours of {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|qid=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō|1=Emperor Keikō}}. Another distinguishing feature is the frequent mention of {{ill|Tsuchigumo|en|Tsuchigumo|qid=Q2063089|lt=Tsuchigumo|1=Tsuchigumo}}. === Origin of the Province Name === According to the Fudoki, when the administrator of the province, Unade, visited Nakatsu District in {{ill|Buzen Province|en|Buzen Province|qid=Q1047134|lt=Buzen Province|1=Buzen Province}} (modern [[:en:Yukuhashi|Yukuhashi]], {{ill|Miyako|en|Miyako|qid=Q355192|lt=Miyako|1=Miyako}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|qid=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}), a white bird appeared. The bird transformed first into rice cakes, then into taro plants, flourishing even in winter. Upon hearing this, Emperor Keikō declared, "These are heavenly blessings, fertile grasses of the land," and named the area "豊国" (Toyokuni, "Abundant Land"). The province was later divided into two, creating Bungo. === District Names and Origins === * '''Hita District''' (日田郡) – Named after the goddess Hisatsu-hime (比佐津媛), who transformed into human form to greet Emperor Keikō during his visit. * '''Kusu District''' (玖珠郡) – Named for a giant camphor tree (樟). * '''Naoiri District''' (直入郡) – Named for a tall, straight mulberry tree, originally called ''Nao-kuba'' (直桑). * '''Ōno District''' (大野郡) – Named for its vast wilderness. * '''Ama District''' (海部郡) – Named for the many ''Ama'' (sea people) living there. * '''Ōita District''' (大分郡) – Emperor Keikō named it "広大" (Seikida, "Broad and Large") for its vast terrain; later simplified to Ōita. * '''Hayami District''' (速見郡) – Named after Queen Hayatsu-hime (速津媛), who greeted Emperor Keikō during his visit. * '''Kunisaki District''' (国埼郡) – Named when Emperor Keikō saw a peninsula from the sea and declared, "That must be the cape of a province!" The readings of district names are based on those recorded in the {{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|en|Wamyō Ruijushō|qid=Q2702542|lt=Wamyō Ruijushō|1=Wamyō Ruijushō}}, transcribed into modern kana usage. === Other Articles of Interest === * Accounts of {{ill|Tsuchigumo|en|Tsuchigumo|qid=Q2063089|lt=Tsuchigumo|1=Tsuchigumo}} extermination, including figures such as Itsuma-hime (五馬姫), Uchi-saru (打猴), and Yata (八田), and locations like Neko-no-Iwaya (鼠の磐窟). * Descriptions of hot springs, including the "Red Spring" (''Akayu no Izumi,'' 赤湯泉, modern {{ill|Chinoike Jigoku|ja|血の池地獄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinoike Jigoku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別府地獄めぐり|1=Hells of Beppu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q5709027}}) and "Kuberi Hot Spring" (玖倍理湯の井, believed to refer to modern {{ill|Kannawa Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa Onsen|1=Kannawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q30921762}}). == Notes == {{reflist|qq=}} == External Links == * {{PDFlink|[http://ir.lib.hiroshima-u.ac.jp/metadb/up/kiyo/AN00176886/naikai_28_v31.pdf Full text of Tsutsui Ennen's ''Bungo no Kuni Fudoki'']|qq=}} by Motonichi Nishibeppu, Hiroshima University Institute for Seto Inland Sea Studies, ''Inland Sea Culture Research Bulletin,'' No. 28, pp. 31–60. * {{NDLDC|1181222|Bungo Kuni Shi: Including Annotated ''Bungo Fudoki'' (Niho Bunko Edition, 1931)|qq=}}. {{Japanese-history-stub|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|豊後国風土記|version=87564998|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:豊後国風土記]]
